research_answer: The chances are surprisingly low. Only 9% of countries that exported raw cotton in 1995 but lacked comparative advantage in processed cotton managed to develop that capability within the next 5 years. This is particularly striking because intuition might suggest that having the raw material gives a country a natural stepping stone into downstream processing. Instead, the data shows that raw cotton exporters are far more likely to diversify laterally — 25% of those without comparative advantage in fruit developed it over the same 5-year window. This suggests that the productive capabilities needed to export raw cotton (resource rights, basic logistics) are more closely related to other agricultural sectors than to textile manufacturing.
supporting_quotes: Furthermore, of the countries that exported raw cotton in 1995 but did not export processed cotton, only 9% were able to gain comparative advantage in processed cotton over the next 5 years.
And one quarter of raw cotton exporters who could not export fruit with comparative advantage in 1995 were able to develop comparative advantage in the fruit sector over the next 5 years.
